description: On your second day, take a local bus to Monachil and go on a waterfall hike  through the mountains and caves of Granada. You will hike to a few waterfalls and across suspension bridges, which are fun and easy micro-adventures you can do from Granada.
Don't forget to walk along the  and see the city from the opposite side of the Daugava River. It's quite a sight to see.
You can spend the rest of the day exploring the  area, a historic old town of Leipzig, where you will be able to find interesting architecture and monuments along the walking path.
Zurich, which is both beautiful and expensive, is the largest city in Switzerland. Places like the  are great places to get a glimpse of what Zurich was like in medieval times.

If you want to get the best view of Paris from above, don't go up the Eiffel Tower! The cityscape of Paris is nothing without the iconic Eiffel Tower, and how would you see it if you are on top of it, right? For the best view of the city, I highly recommend you go up the  instead.

First, if you want to get a nice view of Paris, do not go up the Eiffel Tower. Go up the  instead. It's less crowded, there's no need to line up, and best of all, you get a view of Paris and the Eiffel Tower from there.

Last but not least, if you have a day to spare in Avignon, you have to visit , a historic city known for its rich history that dates all the way back to Roman times.

You can also go on a day trip and explore the surrounding towns like , home to the famous  Amphitheatre; go on a hike at the Fontaine de Vaucluse; or shop at the L'Isle-sur-la-Sorgue Sunday market.

There are several other Roman heritage sites you can check out in Arles, like the , an ancient obelisk dating back to the 1st AD, the Church of St. Trophime, a masterpiece of Romanesque architecture, and the Alyscamps, a Roman necropolis.

The next two days, you can make day trips to  and the Wieliczka Salt Mine, respectively, to learn more about the history of Krakow. Be sure to book these tickets in advance, especially during the peak season.

Getting to the park is a little challenging as you will first have to find the stairs to go up the elevated park. First of all, take the metro to Paris Gare de Lyon station and walk east toward . Along this road, you should find a set of stairs going up the park every few blocks. One of the stairs is shown in the Google Street View below.

Also, don't forget to check out  located right next to the Palais des Papes. The cathedral was built in the 14th century and features a beautiful gilded statue of the Virgin Mary, as well as the tombs of the Avignon Popes inside.

To get from Avignon to L'Isle-sur-la-Sorgue, you can either take the bus or the train from  and you should be there in about 50 minutes by bus or 30 minutes by train. They both run frequently every day and you can book the bus/train ticket here.

To get from Avignon to Arles, you can catch the train from  and you should be there in less than an hour. The train runs rather often but just in case, you can book the train ticket online here.

Everything is within walking distance in Avignon if you decide to stay in the city center. If you want to visit one of the many nearby towns like Arles and Isle-sur-la-Sorgue, you can catch a bus or a train from  to pretty much anywhere in the region, which is very convenient. You can book the train here.

On Tuesday, Thursday, Friday, and Sunday, the street in front of the stunning  is converted into Les Puces de Saint Michel, a vintage market where you can stroll around and acquire some truly unique items as souvenirs.

The second day, go on a hike to the  in Saxony Switzerland National Park. It's a fun nature hike that will allow you to see Germany in a different light. It's the type of place that you wouldn't expect to see in the hyper-modernized cities of Germany.

I made a promise to myself that when I get to Paris, I would do everything I can to visit the eerie , a massive underground cemetery filled with skulls and skeletons that stretch across much of Paris' underground.
While in Torun, be sure to visit , the leaning tower of Torun, and if you want to learn about the Teutonic Knights, be sure to drop by the District Museum.

For the second day,  makes a great day trip from the capital, which is only a 2-hour bus ride away.  is a beautiful old town known for its medieval castle, featuring wall paintings, dungeons, towers, a romantic castle park, and the old crumbling Lutheran church of Saint John the Baptist. It is a great place to learn a little bit about Latvia during medieval times.

Lucerne is one of the few places I visited as a kid that I fondly remember. Places like the  (Kapellbrücke) and its fiery history, the unique location of the Lion Monument, and the history of the Museggmauer, Lucerne’s Old Town, somehow ignite my memory of my time there when I was young. It's a great place to visit for a day before moving on to Zurich.

place: Chartreuse Notre Dame du Val de Bénédiction
description: There are several tourist attractions you should check out in Villeneuve-lès-Avignon like , a historic Carthusian monastery dating back to the Middle Ages, Fort Saint-André, a 14th-century fortress, Abbey St. Andrew, a small palace with a beautiful garden, and more.

, for example, is a great place to hike to as the ruin is located atop a steep cliff right in the middle of the town. If you look around the corner from the roundabout pictured above, cross the bridge, turn right, follow the road, and on your left, there should be a staircase to take you to the top.
One of the most famous landmarks that defines the city is the giant . Despite its size and interior, you can also climb up to the top of one of the towers and get a 360° view of the city from above.
is an idyllic elevated park, full of trees, built on top of an unused railway infrastructure located on the eastern side of Paris.
If you are visiting the , you can also pay a bit more to have access to the tower where you will be able to see Torun from above, which is simply breathtaking.

Spend a day walking around the square and check out the Poznan Town Hall and its colorful construction houses of Poznan (). Other than that, there's not much else to do other than to slow down a bit and treat yourself, as we have already been traveling through four countries, and you will appreciate how cheap it is in Poland compared to other places we have visited.

Oh Berlin, if only I could stay there forever, but we can't, as we only have 90 days here, so let's make the best out of it. Four nights in Berlin will allow you to see most of the awesome places like the , Checkpoint Charlie, Brandenburg Gate, and the Berlin Cathedral.
After the morning market in L'Isle sur la Sorgue, you can catch a bus and visit , a small town nestled between the mountains of Vaucluse. It's a perfect place to do some exploration and hiking.
You can spend the first day exploring all the historical buildings of Lyon, starting with a climb up the  and visiting the Basilica of Notre-Dame de Fourvière.

For one, places like , Amalienborg, and the Rosenborg Castle will keep you occupied for hours on end. Not to mention the King's Garden and Strøget street, where you can spend more hours walking around, exploring, and shopping until you drop.

The most notable landmarks in the city are the Riga Cathedral, which is a good place to begin your journey. From there, you can branch out to the , St. Peter's Church (make sure to go up the tower), House of the Blackheads, and the Museum of the Occupation of Latvia.

Spend your first day visiting the old town area, where you can find Baroque architecture ranging from the beautiful  to the stunning Dresden Cathedral, all located within walking distance of each other.

Vilnius has quite a lot to offer, and places like the , St. Peter and St. Paul's Church, Vilnius Cathedral, and the independent republic of Užupis are great places to get a feel for Vilnius and its people.
Other places worth checking out are the , Rue Sainte Catherine, and the vintage market at Basilique Saint-Michel.
If you have one more day in Stockholm, I would recommend that you go on a day trip and visit the beautiful  near a small town called Mariefred, only 1.5 hours away from Stockholm.

After the market, you can make your way to the tourist strip of Rue Ste Catherine, but first, you have to check out  or Big Bell in English. It is a famous historical bell and clock tower built in the 15th century as a gate and a part of the city's medieval fortifications, as well as to alarm people in case of fire.

After you have explored the Palais des Papes, you can make your way to , a leafy public garden atop a hill, providing a great vantage point over the Rhone River and Villeneuve lès Avignon, just across the river.

The next day, you can go up the cable car to the , where you will be able to see the mountain range from the top. Make sure to check the weather and book the ticket from their website before arriving to avoid disappointment and long lines.
is a great place to end your first day in Krakow. You can enjoy delicious street food like Zapiekanka (a must-try) and have a nice glass of wine while listening to live Jewish music.

is a small town located east of Avignon, around a 30-minute bus ride away. If you happen to be visiting Avignon on a Sunday or Thursday, I highly recommend you go to the town and check out its incredible Sunday market.

is a traditional-looking, authentic Parisian cafe that was used in one of the scenes in Before Sunset, and it has that romantic vibe that you don't get from new, modern cafes.

You can also spend another day café-hopping, as there are countless cafés, bookstores, and parks that are super unique and devoid of tourists like the , Strada Cafe, Honor Cafe, San Francisco Book Co., and the Coulee Verte Rene Dumont park.

Across the Limmat River, there is a historic site called , an elevated platform where a Roman castle once stood. It is surely a place of great historical significance, but it also serves as a great viewpoint for us tourists to see Zurich, the Limmat, and the cathedral from above.
Gdansk is also famous for its colorful architecture around its  district, which is now home to Accidental-Wes-Anderson-style houses and shops.
From Gdansk, you can take a train directly to  in the early morning and spend half a day there.  and its castle are one of the most majestic places you will see in Poland.
Looking to buy something? Be sure to drop by the  or walk along the Königstraße (King Street), a shopping street where you can buy all sorts of snacks or souvenirs for people back home.
There are also places like the , the Hamburg Rathaus City Hall, and the Elbtunnel (an old underground tunnel) that are worth visiting when you are there.

Before we explore the winding streets of Bordeaux, you'd better make your way across the road from Place de la Bourse and witness one of the most beautiful viewpoints in the city: the , a large shallow reflecting pool, one of the largest of its kind.

One of my favorite pieces of architecture in France is the  and the Place de la Bourse in Bordeaux. The  is the largest water mirror in the world and is located right by the 18th-century Place de la Bourse.

is quite a sight to behold. It is a fortified island located in Normandy with over 60 buildings in the commune. Its fairy-tale-like architecture, unique location, and the constructed commune inside make walking around it feel like being in a fantasy novel or something.
The next day, you can go on a day trip and visit the , which is located at the top of a rocky mountain. It is only 1.5 hours away from Barcelona by train.
There are other things you can do in Fontaine de Vaucluse, like visiting the , the Museum Bibliothèque François Pétrarque, and plenty more that should occupy you for the rest of the day.
If you want to learn a bit more about Bordeaux and its rich history, you can also check out the  while you are here, as it is located just opposite the palace.

Of course, you can't visit Paris without visiting the . Although much of the main hall is under renovation due to fire, causing its ceiling to collapse, much of the facade and its towers remain standing, and you can still admire the beauty of this iconic landmark.
The most popular place to visit in Tallinn is the , where you will find several beautiful architectural structures, such as the Tallinn Town Hall, surrounding the area.

The square is also known for its classical and elegant 18th-century architecture surrounding it. One of the most notable buildings on the square you should check out is the  and admire its incredible Neoclassical-style façade, adorned with columns and sculptures.

One of the most famous landmarks in Avignon and one that you should visit first is , also known as the "Palace of the Popes". It is one of the most significant Gothic-style palaces in Europe, serving as the residence for several popes during the 14th century when the papal court was moved to Avignon from Rome in 1309.
I low-key love traveling in Avignon. It is a stunning city full of antiquity, medieval castles, and old towns to explore. The most stunning one of all is the  located in the heart of the city.

If reading the long and rich history of Avignon is not something you find interesting, you can still learn about the history of the place with a nightly light show (Les Luminessences), which is often held from August to October, right after sunset, in the .
If you want a nice panoramic view of the city, head over to  near the old town. Pay 3 EUR, and you will be able to access its rooftop platform and get a nice photo of Leipzig from above.
is a great place to start your day with a hike to the top of the hill to see Barcelona from above. This is also where the iconic Gaudí's House Museum is located.

One of the first places you should visit when you are in Bordeaux is  - the main city square where the roads to all the tourist attractions converge, which makes for a great place to start your exploration.

Spend the next full day and walk to  in the morning, cool down at Maria Luisa Park, and make your way back through Alcázar of Seville and its beautiful gardens inside, and the Catedral de Sevilla, a giant Gothic-style church, the third largest in the world.

If you want a great view of the Seine River and the Eiffel Tower, the  is a great spot to stop by while you explore Paris. This 19th-century arched bridge is designed in an extravagant Beaux-Arts style with intricate golden sculptures on both ends, all lined up with elaborately designed lamps.
With the Palais des Papes' ticket in hand, given that you bought the 14.5 EUR one, you should be able to get access to the  bridge, one of the most famous landmarks in the city.
After you are done with Miroir d'eau, you can walk along the river and cross the  Stone Bridge for a nice view of the city over the Garonne River.
Right near the Place de la Bourse is the 18th-century  that connects the left bank of the Garonne River to the right bank Quartier de la Bastide, which is also worth visiting.

After you are done with the bridge, it is time to explore Bordeaux proper, and the first piece of history you will encounter right off the gate is , a castle-like gate that was built in the late 15th century as the main entry point to the city.
If it's raining outside, make it a museum day and visit the  to learn more about the history of the Dutch people. If you are into art, the Van Gogh Museum is a must-visit.

To get from L'Isle sur la Sorgue to Fontaine de Vaucluse, you can catch bus #921 from  and get off at Fontaine Centre bus stop. It should take no more than 15 minutes and cost around 2 EUR. The last bus is at 6 PM, so be sure to get there in time, given you are there in the summer months. Off-season is not recommended as the bus schedule will not work for day trips like what we are doing.

Arles was once an important Roman provincial capital known as Arelate, and one of the most important landmarks that you have to check out first is the  (Arènes d'Arles), a large amphitheater that could seat over 20,000 spectators. It was built in 90 AD and is still used for events even today.

MADRide provides a free walking tour every day at the famous meeting point, Puerta del Sol Square, and the guide will take you to cool places like the , Catedral de la Almudena, and Plaza Mayor while explaining the stories behind these places.

One of the main tourist strips of Bordeaux, , is best known for being one of the longest pedestrian shopping streets in Europe, with a total length of approximately 1.2 kilometers (0.75 miles). It stretches from Place de la Victoire to Place de la Comédie.

There are also many other bookstores that aren't as crowded as Shakespeare and Company, like  which holds many awesome secondhand collections, such as old copies of Asimov's sci-fi novels that I had been tracking down for a while.

The first place you should visit in Stuttgart is the , one of the largest and most impressive squares in the city. Around the area, you will find several historical buildings, restaurants, and cafes where you can indulge yourself.

For a half-day trip, you can visit the 18th Century , where you can hike around the island and explore all the attractions. The best route to walk is the Blue Route. I would recommend following the main "Blue Route", which should take you to all the main sites such as the King's Gate and the Kustaanmiekka artillery.

The city is filled with amazing independent bookstores, including one of the oldest ones of all, . If you love old books, you are going to love this iconic bookstore that every hipster and their grandmothers know about.
Be sure to go up the  so that you can get a 360° panoramic view of Nuremberg from above.
stretches over 1.6 km alongside a canal, and with its unique red-brick buildings crisscrossing the canals, it makes the area a perfect place to walk around and admire the architecture.

Spend your first day in the city center and check out the stunning interior of the  and all the attractions surrounding the square, such as the Town Hall Tower and Krakow Barbican. Then, walk a little further south to Wawel Castle.
, Alexander Nevsky Cathedral, Toompea Castle, and St Mary's Cathedral are definitely worth seeing, but my favorite place has to be the Kohtuotsa and Patkuli viewing platforms.

Most of the cool places to visit are concentrated in the Marienplatz area, so spend a day exploring that district. Go up the  to get a nice view of Munich from above or visit the Nymphenburg Palace for half a day.

The most notable landmarks in the city are the Riga Cathedral, which is a good place to begin your journey. From there, you can branch out to the Freedom Square,  (make sure to go up the tower), House of the Blackheads, and the Museum of the Occupation of Latvia.
Be sure to drop by , Gamla Stan (The Old Town), and be at the Royal Palace around 12:15 PM to witness the Changing of the Guard ceremony.
There are several charming corners of Stockholm that you will like as well, such as the , all the alleyways leading up to the German Church, and the Drottninggatan Street.
If you are looking for a nice lookout point, make sure that you make it all the way up to  or the Killesberg Tower, where you will be able to capture a panoramic view of Stuttgart from above.
If you take the TGV train to , it is 2 km away from the city, and you are going to have to take another train/bus to the city center. You can book the train here.
Be sure to go up the  for a fee, and you will have a 360-degree view of the city. The best time to go up is right before the sun sets.
is also a must-visit when you are in Barcelona. Make sure to check out the Cathedral of Barcelona, its beautiful Roman-era buildings, and all the antique narrow streets in the area.

is one of the most iconic landmarks in the city and it is easily walkable from the city center. The same goes for other famous tourist attractions like Uspenski Cathedral and the stunning architecture of Helsinki Central Station.
From Munich, you can take a bus to Nuremberg, a place where you can experience German history up close.  is the highlight of Nuremberg and a must-visit when you are there.
If you are into cafes as much as I am, there are plenty of cafes in Copenhagen that truly master the art of Hygge, like  and The Bastard Cafe waiting for you to experience.

around Wroclaw Town Hall is the main square where all the stunning Gothic-style architecture and colorful buildings are. However, my favorite area is Ostrów Tumski, a quiet old district marked by the beautiful Cathedral of St. John the Baptist. The peaceful vibe of the area drew me in right away, and I highly recommend staying here for as long as you can.

place: The National Museum of Natural History
description: in Paris is another fun museum to visit if you are interested in learning more about the natural history of the world.

is the landmark of the city with a spacious walking area, towers, colorful traditional architecture, and churches all connected through roads leading to the Old Town marketplace. While there, be sure to check out Sigismund's Column, the Royal Castle, and St. John's Archcathedral.

If you are interested in learning about Germany and its dark past, I highly recommend you visit  and DDR museum. These museums will give you a detailed look into Germany during the Nazi regime and the Soviet occupation that followed, respectively.

If you are looking to go out at night, check out  and align your visit with their event schedule for a nice night out. Located by the Spree river, this artistic space is dedicated to urban cultures through exhibitions, art, DIY workshops, and concerts.

If you are there on a rainy/snowy day, use it to your advantage and visit all the cool museums in Stockholm. Two museums I would recommend you visit are the , where the giant renovated Viking ship is located, to learn about the Vasa ship, and the Nordic Museum to learn about Nordic history in extreme detail.
At the outskirts of Avignon, just across the Rhone river to the north, lies a medieval town called , one of the hidden gems of Avignon that few people know about.
Or, you can simply relax, go for a walk around the  and enjoy the fact that you have just traveled across 10+ countries in 3 months and seen more than anyone could ask for on such a trip. Kudos!

First, you should visit the Monument to the Battle of the Nations (), a monument commemorating Napoleon's defeat at Leipzig, which was a crucial step towards the end of the war. The monument is one of the highest of its kind in Europe.

If you want to learn about the Polish experience during World War II, visit , an open-air museum dedicated to the Battle of . This battle was the first clash between Polish and German forces during the invasion of Poland and, therefore, the initial battle of World War II in Europe.
For the second day, we will take it a little slower and take a bus heading towards Wilanow from the Old Square and get off at the end of the line where the  is located.
From Seville, it's only a 3-hour bus ride away until you arrive in Granada. There are several notable things you can do in Granada, such as hiking, visiting , and eating delicious tapas.

There are other secrets too, such as visiting  on a Tuesday (when it's closed), so you can have the whole place to yourself if you don't mind not seeing the Mona Lisa. You can also go down a dark path and explore the Catacombs of Paris.

place: the bus stop in front of the Post Office
description: The town is approximately 3 km away, and you can catch local bus #5 from , which is just opposite the train station. Get off at the tourist office there. It should take less than 15 minutes.
After spending a few hours at the Palace, on your way back to the city center, be sure to visit , the Palace on the Isle, and the Uprising Museum.
